questions.question: Why does naive quantum error correction sometimes fail?

options.text: The codes are not strong enough
Gates used for syndrome measurement can introduce more errors than the code can correct

questions.explanation: Naive QEC fails because the syndrome measurement circuits themselves use imperfect gates. A single error during syndrome extraction can propagate to multiple data qubits, overwhelming the code's correction capacity. This is why fault-tolerant design is essential.

questions.question: What does it mean for a procedure to be fault-tolerant for a distance-d code?

options.text: A single fault causes at most ⌊(d-1)/2⌋ errors on each code block

questions.explanation: A fault-tolerant procedure ensures that a single fault (gate error, measurement error, etc.) causes at most ⌊(d-1)/2⌋ errors per code block. This guarantees the code can still correct all errors from a single fault.

questions.question: How does an X error on the control qubit of a CNOT propagate?

options.text: It stays only on the control
It moves only to the target
It copies to the target (both qubits now have X errors)
It converts to a Z error

questions.explanation: An X error on the control of CNOT propagates to the target: X⊗I before CNOT becomes X⊗X after CNOT. This is why a single X error on an ancilla controlling multiple CNOTs can create errors on multiple data qubits.

questions.question: What is the purpose of verifying ancilla states before use in syndrome measurement?

options.text: To detect if errors occurred during ancilla preparation
To entangle the ancilla with data

questions.explanation: Verification checks if any errors occurred during ancilla preparation. If verification fails, the ancilla is discarded and preparation is retried. This prevents faulty ancillas from corrupting the syndrome measurement.

questions.question: Why are syndrome measurements typically repeated multiple times?

options.text: To make the measurement more precise
To filter out transient measurement errors

questions.explanation: Repeating syndrome measurements (typically d times for distance-d code) allows filtering of transient measurement errors. A measurement error appears as a syndrome that changes between rounds, while a persistent data error shows consistent syndromes.

questions.question: What is the logical error rate scaling with fault tolerance for a distance-d code?
questions.explanation: With fault tolerance, the logical error rate scales as p_L ∝ (c·p)^⌊(d+1)/2⌋. For a distance-3 code, it's ~p²; for distance-5, it's ~p³. This exponential suppression with distance is the power of fault-tolerant QEC.

questions.question: Transversal gates are automatically fault-tolerant because an error on one physical qubit cannot spread to other qubits within the same code block.
questions.explanation: True. Transversal gates apply the same operation to each physical qubit independently. Since there are no gates coupling qubits within a code block, an error on one qubit stays on that qubit and cannot spread to others.
